1: Nehemiah the governor (hattiršāṯāʾ) heads the list of covenant-signers. That the civil governor signs first — before the priests — reflects the integrated nature of the restoration community's authority structure. Nehemiah's signing represents the full weight of civic governance committed to covenant obedience. In the new covenant, Christ himself is the ultimate civil-priestly authority who mediates the covenant — 'the mediator of a new covenant' (Hebrews 9:15) who signs it not with ink but with his own blood (Luke 22:20).
2: Seraiah the priest is listed first among the priestly signers. The priestly community's formal covenant commitment demonstrates that the temple apparatus is fully aligned with the covenant's demands. The priestly role in the new covenant is fulfilled in Christ, the great high priest who 'offered himself once to bear the sins of many' (Hebrews 9:28) and whose single act replaces the ongoing priestly covenant-maintenance of the old order.
3: Amariah, Malchijah, Pashur — three priestly families committing to covenant faithfulness. Their individual signatures matter: each family's participation signifies that the priestly institution as a whole is bound to the covenant. In the new covenant, the entire community of believers is a 'royal priesthood' (1 Peter 2:9) — not a specialized class but all those united to the great high priest.
4: Hattush, Shebaniah, Malluch — more priestly signers. The list's detail serves the same function as the Ezra 2 census: names matter covenantally. To be named in the covenant document is to be identified as belonging to the covenant people. The NT parallel is the 'book of life' (Philippians 4:3; Revelation 21:27) — the eschatological covenant register in which the names of those who belong to Christ are written.
5: Harim, Meremoth, Obadiah — the priestly list continues. Meremoth son of Uriah had received the temple vessels from Ezra's caravan (Ezra 8:33) and repaired sections of the wall (Nehemiah 3:4, 21); his multiple roles demonstrate how the same individuals served across both the cultic and civic dimensions of the restoration. Christ encompasses both: 'in him all the fullness of God was pleased to dwell' (Colossians 1:19) — the one in whom every restoration role is concentrated and fulfilled.
6: Daniel, Ginnethon, Baruch — continuing the priestly registry. Baruch was a common name in the restoration period (cf. Baruch son of Neriah, Jeremiah's secretary, Jeremiah 32:12). Each named individual represents a household, an extended family, a lineage that is now formally committed to the covenant's demands. The formal commitment of named individuals to covenant obedience is the old covenant's way of achieving what the new covenant achieves through the work of the Spirit in the heart.
7: Meshullam, Abijah, Mijamin — Abijah's priestly division is the one in which Zechariah the father of John the Baptist served (Luke 1:5), making this family's covenant signature in Nehemiah 10 a link in the chain that leads directly to the forerunner of the Messiah. The small, named family of Abijah signing a covenant document in Jerusalem carries the thread that will produce the voice 'crying in the wilderness' (Isaiah 40:3; Mark 1:3).
8: Maaziah, Bilgai, Shemaiah — completing the priestly signers. The list of 21 priestly names is followed by the Levitical and lay leaders, showing that the covenant commitment is comprehensive — all three estates of the covenant community (priestly, Levitical, lay leadership) formally join the document. This comprehensiveness anticipates the new covenant's reach: 'they shall all know me, from the least of them to the greatest' (Jeremiah 31:34; Hebrews 8:11).
9: The Levitical signers begin with Jeshua son of Azaniah, Binnui of the sons of Henadad, and Kadmiel — three leading Levitical families of the restoration period. Kadmiel appears in the Ezra 2 return list, the wall-building narratives, and the worship leadership of Neh 9; his signature here connects the entire Ezra-Nehemiah narrative to this formal covenant commitment. The Levites were the community's teachers and liturgical leaders — their formal covenant commitment was essential for the ongoing instruction and worship that sustained covenant life.
10: Sherebiah, Hodijah, Kelita, Pelaiah, Hanan — Levitical leaders. Sherebiah was one of the key Levites who helped Ezra read and explain the Torah (Nehemiah 8:7) and led the confession of Nehemiah 9; his signature here connects the teaching office to the covenantal commitment. The Levitical teachers who explain the law also bind themselves to its demands — their personal covenant faithfulness is inseparable from their instructional role.
11: Micha, Rehob, Hashabiah — more Levitical signers. Hashabiah appears in Nehemiah 11:15 as an overseer of the Levites in Jerusalem and in 12:24 as a Levitical leader of thanksgiving; the covenant document names those who will implement the commitments of vv30-39 in daily institutional life. The new covenant's teachers are similarly those who are 'transformed by the renewing of your mind' (Romans 12:2) before they teach — formation precedes instruction.
12: Zaccur, Sherebiah, Shebaniah — the Levitical list continues. Multiple individuals named Sherebiah appear in the restoration records, showing how names recur across priestly and Levitical families; each instance is a distinct person and household adding their commitment to the covenant document.
13: Hodijah, Bani, Beninu — completing the named Levitical signers. The Levitical list of 17 names represents the teaching and musical infrastructure of the post-exilic community; their formal covenant commitment ensures that the community's liturgical and educational apparatus is not merely performing rituals but personally bound to the covenant they administer.
14: The lay leaders begin: Parosh, Pahath-moab, Elam, Zattu, Bani — all families from the first-return census of Ezra 2 and Neh 7. The lay leaders' covenant commitment extends the document beyond the religious professionals into the economic and social life of the community. The new covenant similarly reaches beyond cultic observance into the whole of life: 'whether you eat or drink, or whatever you do, do all to the glory of God' (1 Corinthians 10:31).
15: Bunni, Azgad, Bebai — more lay families from the census lists. The appearance of the same family names in the return lists, the wall-building accounts, and now the covenant document traces the continuity of specific families across the entire Ezra-Nehemiah narrative — the same households who returned under Zerubbabel, rebuilt under Nehemiah, and now commit themselves to the covenant form the institutional backbone of the restoration community.
16: Adonijah, Bigvai, Adin — three more prominent lay families. Their participation in the covenant document signals that the wealthy and influential members of the community are formally bound to the same Torah obligations as the priests and Levites — no social class is exempt from the covenant's demands. Christ similarly makes no social distinction in his call: 'Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden' (Matthew 11:28).
17: Ater, Hezekiah, Azzur — continuing the lay leaders. The diversity of family backgrounds represented in the covenant document (farmers, traders, administrators, returning exiles from different Babylonian communities) reflects the social breadth of the covenant commitment. The same breadth characterizes the new covenant community: 'There is neither Jew nor Greek, slave nor free, male nor female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus' (Galatians 3:28).
18: Hodijah, Hashum, Bezai — three more families. Hashum's descendants had been among those who had married foreign wives and were required to divorce them under Ezra's reform (Ezra 10:33); now they are among the covenant signers committing to exactly the prohibition their family had previously violated. The presence of repentant families among the covenant signers shows that the document is not merely a confirmation of the already faithful but a commitment by those who know their own history of failure.
19: Hariph, Anathoth, Nebai — the name Anathoth here refers to a family rather than the town. Anathoth as a family name carries the association of the town of Jeremiah — the prophet whose entire ministry was devoted to calling Israel back to covenant faithfulness; to bear the name Anathoth while signing the covenant document is to embody the call Jeremiah spent his life making.
20: Magpiash, Meshullam, Hezir — three more lay leaders. Meshullam is a common name in the restoration community (at least 17 individuals bear this name across Ezra-Nehemiah); its recurrence shows the density of these lay leadership families and their consistent presence across every major institutional act of the restoration — return, wall-building, covenant-signing.
21: Meshezabel, Zadok, Jaddua — Jaddua is notable as also the name of the last high priest mentioned in the OT (Nehemiah 12:11); whether this is the same individual or a different person bearing the same name, the name Jaddua appears at both the beginning and end of the post-exilic priestly succession, framing the entire restoration period.
22: Pelatiah, Hanan, Anaiah — the lay leader list continues. Hanan appears in multiple roles in Nehemiah's administration (one of the storeroom treasurers in Nehemiah 13:13); the individual who manages the material resources of the community is also personally signed to the covenant — administrative faithfulness and covenantal commitment are integrated, not separated.
23: Hoshea, Hananiah, Hashub — Hananiah is the name of multiple people in Nehemiah's Jerusalem, including the wall-builder (Nehemiah 3:8) and the governor of the citadel (Nehemiah 7:2). The multiplication of the same name across the restoration community shows that these are covenant names — names that mean 'YHWH is gracious' and 'YHWH has been gracious' — chosen by families who wished to identify themselves with the grace of the God whose covenant they were now formally entering.
24: Hallohesh, Pilha, Shobek — Hallohesh ('the whisperer/enchanter') is an unusual name for a covenant signer; its presence reminds that the covenant document includes those with unexpected backgrounds. The new covenant similarly reaches those who come from surprising places: 'God chose what is low and despised in the world, even things that are not, to bring to nothing things that are, so that no human being might boast in the presence of God' (1 Corinthians 1:28-29).
25: Rehum, Hashabnah, Maaseiah — three more signers completing the lay list. The name Maaseiah ('work of YHWH') appears more than 20 times in the OT; its frequency in the restoration community suggests it was favored as a statement of identity — the people who bore this name identified themselves as 'YHWH's work,' which is precisely what they were as a restored community reconstituted by divine grace.
26: Ahijah, Hanan, Anan — three more lay leaders. Ahijah means 'brother of YHWH' — a name that carries the covenant relationship language of kinship; to be YHWH's brother is to be in the closest possible covenant bond. The new covenant makes this metaphorical kinship concrete: 'he is not ashamed to call them brothers' (Hebrews 2:11) — Christ himself takes the title that Ahijah's family bore as their covenant identity.
27: Malluch, Harim, Baanah — the final three lay signers. With 84 named individuals across vv1-27 (Nehemiah + 83 others), the covenant document is comprehensively attested. The 84 signers represent the full institutional structure of the restoration community: governor, priests, Levites, and lay leaders. The comprehensiveness of the signing mirrors the comprehensiveness of the new covenant: 'from the least of them to the greatest, they shall all know me' (Jeremiah 31:34).
28: 'The rest of the people... everyone who had knowledge and understanding, entered into a curse and an oath to walk in God's Law that was given by Moses the servant of God.' The community joining the covenant beyond the named signers shows that the document is not merely elite commitment but encompasses the whole people — servants, gatekeepers, temple singers, those 'separated from the peoples of the lands' to join Israel. This inclusive joining anticipates the new covenant's reach to all who respond: 'whoever believes in him should not perish but have eternal life' (John 3:16) — the covenant entry is open to any who will come.
29: 'They joined with their brothers, their nobles, and entered into a curse and an oath to walk in God's Law that was given by Moses the servant of God, and to observe and do all the commandments of the LORD our Lord and his rules and his statutes.' The self-imposed curse for covenant violation is the old covenant's enforcement mechanism — the community calls divine punishment on itself if it breaks the terms. Christ absorbed this covenant curse: 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us' (Galatians 3:13). The curse that the Nehemiah community placed on themselves for future potential violations, Christ bore as a present reality for past actual violations.
30: 'We will not give our daughters to the peoples of the land or take their daughters for our sons.' The anti-intermarriage covenant commitment addresses the cultural assimilation that had led to every previous generation's apostasy. The theological principle behind it — the covenant community maintains its distinct identity and orientation — is preserved in the new covenant not through ethnic separation but through the transformation of the heart: 'Do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers' (2 Corinthians 6:14) is the new covenant application of the same principle, extended from ethnic identity to shared faith in Christ.
31: 'And if the peoples of the land bring in goods or any grain on the Sabbath day to sell, we will not buy from them on the Sabbath or on a holy day. And we will forego the crops of the seventh year and the exaction of every debt.' The sabbatical-year and debt-release commitments point toward the Jubilee — YHWH's built-in economic reset for a society prone to permanent stratification. Jesus' inaugural sermon announces 'the year of the Lord's favor' (Luke 4:19, citing Isaiah 61:1-2) — the ultimate Jubilee in which all debts are forgiven, all captives released, all the poor given good news. The commitment of Nehemiah 10:31 is the old covenant's approximation; Christ's announcement is the new covenant's fulfillment.
32: 'We also take on ourselves the obligation to give yearly a third part of a shekel for the service of the house of our God.' The temple tax — Moses had prescribed a half-shekel ransom (Exodus 30:13), here reduced to a third-shekel by community agreement — funded the community's shared worship infrastructure. Matthew records that Christ paid the temple tax 'so that we do not give offense to them' (Matthew 17:27) — not because he was obligated but as a voluntary act of solidarity. Christ pays the ultimate 'ransom for many' (Matthew 20:28), the price that no coin could cover.
33: 'The showbread, the regular grain offering, the regular burnt offering, the Sabbaths, the new moons, the appointed feasts, the holy things, and the sin offerings to make atonement for Israel' — the full liturgical calendar funded by the temple tax. Every item on this list points toward Christ: the showbread anticipates the bread of his presence (John 6:51); the burnt offering prefigures his total self-offering (Hebrews 10:5-10); the sin offering is fulfilled in 'God made him who had no sin to be sin for us' (2 Corinthians 5:21); the appointed feasts find their fulfillment in him as Passover, Firstfruits, and Tabernacles.
34: 'We, the priests, the Levites, and the people, have likewise cast lots for the wood offering, to bring it into the house of our God... to burn on the altar of the LORD our God.' The wood offering supplied fuel for the altar fire — the continuous sacrifice required continuously burning wood. The fire was first lit by YHWH himself (Leviticus 9:24; 2 Chronicles 7:1), a divine fire that the community was commanded to keep burning. Christ is the one who 'baptizes with the Holy Spirit and fire' (Matthew 3:11); the divine fire that fell on Pentecost (Acts 2:3) is the new covenant's fulfillment of the continuous fire — not a temple altar but the community of believers as the temple in which divine fire burns.
35: 'We obligate ourselves to bring the firstfruits of our ground and the firstfruits of all fruit of every tree, year by year, to the house of the LORD.' Firstfruits acknowledged that the entire harvest belonged to YHWH and that the community's abundance was gift, not achievement. Paul identifies Christ as 'the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep' (1 Corinthians 15:20, 23) — the first of the resurrection harvest that will include all who belong to him. The commitment to bring firstfruits to YHWH's house anticipates the community's recognition that Christ's resurrection is the firstfruits of the eschatological harvest of the dead.
36: 'As it is written in the Law, we will bring the firstborn of our sons and of our cattle, as it is written in the Law, and the firstborn of our herds and of our flocks to the house of our God.' The firstborn belongs to YHWH — redeemed from death by sacrifice. Christ is 'the firstborn over all creation' (Colossians 1:15) and 'the firstborn from among the dead' (Colossians 1:18; Revelation 1:5) — not merely a firstborn who requires redemption but the firstborn who is himself the means of redemption. He is the firstborn whom death could not hold, whose resurrection makes possible the redemption of all whom death holds.
37: 'We will bring the first of our dough, our contributions, the fruit of every tree, the wine and the oil, to the priests, to the chambers of the house of our God; and we will bring the tithes from our ground to the Levites, for the Levites collect the tithes in all our towns where we labor.' The comprehensive system of tithes and offerings funded the covenant community's worship and instruction. Paul uses this system as the basis for supporting new covenant ministers (1 Corinthians 9:13-14) and as a metaphor for generous giving (2 Corinthians 9:6-15). Christ, as the great high priest, receives the ultimate 'tithe' — not a tenth of income but the whole life offered in living sacrifice (Romans 12:1).
38: 'And the priest, the son of Aaron, shall be with the Levites when the Levites receive the tithes. And the Levites shall bring up the tithe of the tithes to the house of our God, to the chambers of the storehouse.' The tithe of tithes — the Levites' tithe of what they receive — creates a chain of giving from the whole people through the Levites to the priests: 'Bring the full tithe into the storehouse, that there may be food in my house' (Malachi 3:10). The 'storehouse' of the new covenant temple is the community itself (1 Corinthians 3:16; Ephesians 2:21-22), built and sustained by Christ as the great high priest whose provision never runs out.
39: 'For the people of Israel and the sons of Levi shall bring the contribution of grain, wine, and oil to the chambers where the vessels of the sanctuary are, where the priests who minister, and the gatekeepers and the singers are. We will not neglect the house of our God.' The closing pledge — 'we will not neglect the house of our God' — is the positive summation of every covenant commitment in the chapter. In the new covenant, 'the house of God' is not a temple building but the assembled community (1 Timothy 3:15; Hebrews 10:21-25): 'not neglecting to meet together, as is the habit of some, but encouraging one another, and all the more as you see the Day drawing near' (Hebrews 10:25). The pledge to not neglect the house of God reaches its final form in the mutual commitment of believers to the body of Christ.
